analogy: Think of "Arts of the Mauryan Period" through the chapter's own opening images: SIXTH century BCE marks the beginning of new religious and social movements in the Gangetic valley in the form of Buddhism and Jainism which were part of the shraman tradition. Both religions became popular as they opposed the varna and jati systems of the Hindu... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Arts of the Mauryan Period is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that sIXTH century BCE marks the beginning of new religious and social movements in the Gangetic valley in the form of Buddhism and Jainism which were part of the shraman tradition.

The text explains that both religions became popular as they opposed the varna and jati systems of the Hindu religion.

The text explains that magadha emerged as a powerful kingdom and consolidated its control over the other regions.

The text explains that by the fourth century BCE the Mauryas established their power and by the third century BCE, a large part of India was under Mauryan control.

The text explains that ashoka emerged as the most powerful king of the Mauryan dynasty who patronised the Buddhist shraman tradition in the third century BCE.

The text explains that religious practices had many dimensions and were not confined to just one particular mode of worship.

The text explains that worship of Yakshas and mother-goddesses were prevalent during that time.

The text explains that nevertheless, Buddhism became the most popular social and religious movement.

The text explains that yaksha worship was very popular before and after the advent of Buddhism and it was assimilated in Buddhism and Jainism.

The text explains that pillars, Sculptures and Rock-cut Architecture Construction of stupas and viharas as part of monastic establishments became part of the Buddhist tradition.

The text explains that however, in this period, apart from stupas and viharas, stone pillars, rock-cut caves and monumental figure sculptures were carved at several places.

The text explains that the tradition of constructing pillars is very old and it may be observed that erection of pillars was prevalent in the Achamenian empire as well.

The text explains that but the Mauryan pillars are different from the Achamenian pillars.

The text explains that the Mauryan pillars are rock- cut pillars thus displaying the carver’s skills, whereas the Achamenian pillars are constructed in pieces by a mason.

The text explains that stone pillars were erected by Ashoka, which have been found in the north Indian part of the Mauryan Empire with inscriptions engraved on them.

The text explains that the top portion of the pillar was carved with capital figures like the bull, the lion, the elephant, etc.

The text explains that monumental images of Yaksha, Yakhinis and animals, pillar columns with capital figures, rock-cut caves belonging to the third century BCE have been found in different parts of India.

The text explains that depiction of a monumental rock-cut elephant at Dhauli in Odisha shows modelling in round with linear rhythm.

The text explains that the rock-cut cave carved at Barabar hills near Gaya in Bihar is known as the Lomus Rishi cave.

The text explains that the interior hall of this cave is rectangular with a circular chamber at the back.

The text explains that it is important to note that the stupas were constructed over the relics of the Buddha at Rajagraha, Vaishali, Vethadipa and Pava in Bihar, Kapilavastu, Allakappa and Ramagrama in Nepal, Kushinagar and Pippalvina in Uttar Pradesh.

The text explains that the abacus has the depiction of a chakra (wheel) having twenty-four spokes in all the four directions and a bull, a horse, an elephant and a lion between every chakra is finely carved.

The text explains that each petal of the lotus is sculpted keeping in mind its density.

The text explains that during the early phase of Buddhism, Buddha is depicted symbolically through footprints, stupas, lotus throne, chakra, etc.

The text explains that this indicates either simple worship, or paying respect, or at times depicts historisisation of life events.

The text explains that gradually narrative became a part of the Buddhist tradition.

The text explains that thus events from the life of the Buddha, the Jataka stories, were depicted on the railings and torans of the stupas.

The text explains that mainly synoptic narrative, continuous narrative and episodic narrative are used in the pictorial tradition.

The text explains that while events from the life of the Buddha became an important theme in all the Buddhist monuments, the Jataka stories also became equally important for sculptural decorations.

The text explains that the main events associated with the Buddha’s life which were frequently depicted were events related to the birth, renunciation, enlightenment, dhammachakra- pravartana, and mahaparinibbana (liberation from the cycle of birth.) Among the Jataka stories that are frequently depicted are Chhadanta Jataka, Vidurpundita Jataka, Ruru Jataka, Sibi Jataka, Vessantara Jataka and Shama Jataka.

The text explains that do you think that the art of making sculptures in India began during the Mauryan period?

The text explains that what was the significance of the stupa and how did stupa architecture develop?

The text explains that which were the four events in the life of the Buddha which have been depicted in different forms of Buddhist art?

Source: NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art, Chapter 3: Arts of the Mauryan Period

prelims_traps: Do not treat ARTS OF THE and MAURYAN PERIOD as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Arts of the Mauryan Period; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Arts of the Mauryan Period through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use ARTS OF THE and MAURYAN PERIOD to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.

AN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 20 Yaksha, Parkham and carved standing on a square or circular abacus.
